metadata.title: Why a MicroRNA Deletion Can Do the Same Damage As Deleting a Tumor Suppressor
metadata.purpose: Vox-explainer slate cut of Cancer Biology candidate 21. Demonstrates that non-protein-coding sequence can act as a tumor suppressor — deleting miR-15/16 de-represses BCL2 and causes CLL through a purely RNA-mediated route.
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL #1F6F5C = miR-15/16 present / BCL2 silenced / apoptosis normal; CRIMSON #BF3339 = miR-15/16 deleted / BCL2 elevated / apoptosis blocked. Never swap mid-film. GOLD = single editor's-pen highlight fill only (never text).
metadata.note: Card exclusions honored: NO microRNA processing pathway detail (Drosha/Dicer), NO oncomiR examples (miR-21), NO lncRNA biology, NO Polycomb/Trithorax, NO microRNA therapeutics beyond one sentence. Manim move: compare. Illustrative numbers in THE EXAMPLE are labeled as such in FACTCHECK.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Pragmatist register (voices/pragmatist/VOICE.md + HAI.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the HAI outro from AUTHOR.MD :: Humanitarians AI
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: A chromosome deletion drives leukemia. The deleted region contains no protein-coding gene — only RNA molecules. No missing protein is found.

card.copy: Why a MicroRNA Deletion Can Cause Leukemia

beats.new_visual_element: [CARD] title with CANCER BIOLOGY eyebrow in TEAL

beats.narration_text: 2002. The deletion at chromosome 13q14 had been documented in chronic lymphocytic leukemia for years. A tumor suppressor was expected in that region — everyone assumed a missing protein.

props.headline: It is 2002. The deletion at chromosome 13q14 had been seen…

beats.scene_description: A karyotype chromosome spread highlighting a chromosome 13 with a visible deletion gap, clinical cytogenetics image, desaturated editorial treatment on aged newsprint, push-in on the deletion band
beats.image_prompt: B02, human karyotype spread with chromosome 13 highlighted showing a visible deletion gap at the long arm, clinical cytogenetics photograph, high contrast black and white, mounted on aged cream newsprint as an editorial plate, flat print reproduction, no labels on other chromosomes

beats.narration_text: Classical deletion logic: remove a tumor suppressor protein, lose the brake, get cancer. This deletion removed no protein — only two RNA molecules. Why can deleting non-protein-coding sequence cause cancer as reliably as deleting a protein?

card.copy: A deletion should remove a tumor suppressor protein.
card.sub: This one removes only RNA molecules. Why does leukemia still follow?

beats.narration_text: Classical model: genes encode proteins, tumor suppressors are proteins that block cancer, delete the gene and you lose the protein brake. This holds for most tumor suppressors — RB1, TP53, APC.

production_viz.label: classical model: gene -> protein -> brake
production_viz.mechanic: left column: 'GENE' chip (teal) arrow down to 'PROTEIN' chip (teal) arrow down to 'BRAKE' (teal, apoptosis normal); right column: 'GENE DELETED' chip (crimson) arrow down to 'NO PROTEIN' (crimson, dashed) arrow down to 'NO BRAKE' (crimson); two columns show the classical deletion model

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] classical model — gene to protein to brake

beats.narration_text: At chromosome 13q14, the deleted region contained no protein-coding gene. What researchers found instead: miR-15 and miR-16 — two RNA molecules, 22 nucleotides each, that never produce protein.

production_viz.label: what the deletion actually removed: two microRNAs, no protein gene
production_viz.mechanic: a horizontal chromosome bar (slate) with a crimson deletion notch; below the notch: 'miR-15' and 'miR-16' chips (crimson, tiny RNA icons); label 'NO PROTEIN-CODING GENE' with a line-through; label '22 nucleotides each' in MONO below the chips

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] chromosome deletion reveals only miR-15 and miR-16

beats.narration_text: MicroRNAs silence target genes by partial complementarity. A microRNA loads into a silencing complex and finds messenger RNAs with matching sequence fragments in their untranslated ends. One microRNA can silence dozens of mRNA targets simultaneously.

production_viz.label: microRNA silencing: RISC + partial complementarity -> many targets
production_viz.mechanic: center: 'miR-15/16' chip (teal) inside 'RISC' oval (slate); three arrows fanning out to the right to three mRNA chips labeled 'BCL2 mRNA', 'target 2', 'target 3' — all silenced (teal checkmarks). Label 'partial complementarity' below the fan

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] RISC complex with miR-15/16 silencing fan of targets

beats.narration_text: The primary target of miR-15 and miR-16 is BCL2 messenger RNA — the anti-apoptotic guardian. In normal B cells, these microRNAs hold BCL2 protein at baseline. A pro-apoptotic signal tips the balance and the cell dies on schedule.

production_viz.label: normal B cell: miR-15/16 suppress BCL2 mRNA -> BCL2 protein low -> apoptosis normal
production_viz.mechanic: left lane labeled 'miR-15/16 PRESENT' (teal banner); arrow from miR-15/16 chip (teal) to BCL2 mRNA chip with suppression bar; BCL2 protein bar is short (teal); apoptosis outcome chip 'NORMAL' (teal) at bottom

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] normal state — miR-15/16 suppress BCL2, apoptosis normal

beats.narration_text: Delete miR-15 and miR-16. RISC loads nothing. BCL2 messenger RNA runs unchecked. BCL2 protein rises 3 to 5 times above normal. The same pro-apoptotic signal arrives — but BCL2 is now abundant enough to absorb it. BAX cannot overcome the elevated guardian. The cell survives indefinitely.

production_viz.label: CLL: miR-15/16 deleted -> BCL2 mRNA unrestrained -> BCL2 protein high -> apoptosis blocked
production_viz.mechanic: right lane labeled 'miR-15/16 DELETED' (crimson banner); RISC oval is empty (dashed, no miRNA); BCL2 mRNA chip has no suppression bar; BCL2 protein bar is tall (crimson); apoptosis outcome chip 'BLOCKED' (crimson) at bottom

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] deleted state — BCL2 rises, apoptosis blocked

beats.narration_text: The two lanes show why a microRNA deletion is functionally equivalent to overexpressing BCL2 directly. The route to elevated BCL2 runs through RNA, not a protein mutation. The downstream consequence is identical.

production_viz.label: side-by-side two-lane comparison: TEAL lane vs CRIMSON lane
production_viz.mechanic: full two-lane graphic: LEFT lane (teal banner 'miR-15/16 PRESENT'): miRNA loaded, BCL2 mRNA suppressed, BCL2 bar low, outcome 'APOPTOSIS NORMAL'; RIGHT lane (crimson banner 'miR-15/16 DELETED'): RISC empty, BCL2 mRNA unrestrained, BCL2 bar tall, outcome 'APOPTOSIS BLOCKED'. Central divider. Both lanes share same row structure for direct comparison.

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] full two-lane comparison — the visual object

beats.narration_text: The 98 percent of the genome that makes no protein still encodes functional brakes. Deleting a non-coding RNA can cause cancer as cleanly as deleting RB1 or TP53. Tumor suppressor function is not limited to protein-coding genes.

card.copy: Non-coding sequence can act as a tumor suppressor.
card.sub: the brake made of RNA works as reliably as the brake made of protein

beats.new_visual_element: [CARD] section card — non-coding as tumor suppressor

beats.narration_text: Clinical consequence: BCL2 overexpression became the molecular diagnosis of CLL. That opened a direct therapeutic path — venetoclax inhibits BCL2 protein directly, targeting the exact molecule the microRNA deletion allowed to accumulate.

production_viz.label: venetoclax targets the BCL2 protein that miRNA deletion released
production_viz.mechanic: a vertical arrow from 'miR-15/16 DELETED' chip (crimson) down to 'BCL2 ELEVATED' chip (crimson) down to 'CLL' chip (crimson); branching right from BCL2: 'VENETOCLAX' chip (teal) with block arrow hitting BCL2 node. Teal label 'direct target' on the venetoclax branch.

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] venetoclax targets BCL2 that miRNA loss released

beats.narration_text: Normal B cells: miR-15 and miR-16 load into RISC and bind BCL2 messenger RNA. BCL2 protein sits at baseline — 1 unit, illustrative. A pro-apoptotic signal arrives: BAX tips the balance, MOMP occurs, cell dies.

production_viz.label: illustrative example: 100 normal B cells, BCL2=1, BAX wins, cells die
production_viz.mechanic: two stacked rows: top row 'miR-15/16' (teal chips) -> 'RISC' -> 'BCL2 mRNA suppressed'; bottom row 'BCL2 = 1 unit' (small teal bar) | 'BAX WINS' -> 'MOMP' -> 'cell dies (teal)'. Label 'ILLUSTRATIVE' in small MONO font at bottom right.

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] illustrative example — normal B cells die on schedule

beats.narration_text: CLL group: chromosome 13 deletion removed both microRNA genes. BCL2 mRNA runs free. BCL2 protein is 3 to 5 times normal — 4 units, illustrative. Same pro-apoptotic signal. BAX cannot overcome the elevated guardian. Cells accumulate indefinitely.

production_viz.label: illustrative example: CLL B cells, BCL2=4 units, BAX fails, cells accumulate
production_viz.mechanic: two stacked rows: top row 'miR-15/16 DELETED' (crimson chips with strikethrough) -> 'RISC empty' (dashed) -> 'BCL2 mRNA unrestrained'; bottom row 'BCL2 = 4 units' (tall crimson bar) | 'BAX LOSES' -> 'NO MOMP' -> 'cells accumulate' (crimson). Label 'ILLUSTRATIVE' in small MONO font at bottom right.

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] illustrative example — CLL B cells accumulate

beats.narration_text: No protein was deleted. The RNA brake that held BCL2 in check was deleted. Without that brake, BCL2 protein rose and the clone accumulated. Non-coding RNA was the tumor suppressor.

card.copy: Deleting non-protein-coding sequence caused leukemia by releasing BCL2 from its RNA brake.

beats.new_visual_element: [CARD] endcard — question to answer, one line

beats.name: OutroSeries
beats.narration_text: This is part of the Cancer Biology series from Humanitarians AI.

props.tagline: AI education for humanitarian and social-impact practitioners.
